body.title: Igor's input assembly echoes the same thread history dozens of times in his prompt
body.result: write_thread_anchor now strips the synthetic thread-context prefix from user_input before snippeting. Mirrors the strip pattern thalamus already uses — anchored on the last message-tag. 6 tests. Anchor deposits made before this fix retain their nested prefixes but will decay naturally (no cleanup — harmless with the recursion broken). Igor restart to activate. Commit 659bb5a4.
body.description:
Igor self-reported at 2026-04-18 19:22:15:

  'The input repetition here is extreme — the same thread history is echoed dozens of times. That's the loop-closure problem you mentioned, on the input side. Something in the message assembly isn't deduplicating before it hits me.'

He flagged the same issue again at 19:25:50:

  'The input duplication here is severe — the same thread is echoed dozens of times. Worth flagging again that this is getting worse, not better.'

## Scope
This is INPUT-side echo — distinct from the output-side echo silenced by T-scope-guard-echo-dedup (d121605f). The output fix dedup'd channel POSTS. This one affects what lands in Igor's LLM PROMPT: the thread history / message context assembly is concatenating duplicates before sending to inference.

Suspect paths:
1. cortex.twm_push or the TWM renderer — maybe the same thread message is being pushed to TWM on every turn instead of once, and the render pass emits the full list.
2. build_twm_context / _render_twm_snapshot / _render_ring_context in inference_gateway.py — one of these may be dedup-less when assembling the prompt context.
3. A subscription callback in CommsModule may be re-processing the same inbound message multiple times if multiple subscribers fire.
4. thread_anchor memory deposits may be accumulating — a SELECT that returns N copies of the same anchor node.

## First steps
- Instrument build_twm_context / inference prompt assembly to dump what's actually sent to the LLM for one turn
- Count how many times any given message appears in that dump
- If duplicates found, back-trace to the assembly source

## Success criteria
- Igor's prompt assembly contains each distinct message at most once per section
- Igor self-reports the echo is gone on subsequent turns
- No regression in thread-context quality (we want dedup, not amnesia)

## Provenance
Self-reported by Igor-wild-0001 during session 2026-04-18b, twice. Confirmed by Akien on the 19:32:16 channel exchange where he replied 'Claude is working on your echo chamber now.' That context referred to the output dedup; this follow-up ticket addresses the input-side echo.
